queryPrefix: Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Triads
chunks.text:
## Triads
A triad is a three-note chord built by stacking two thirds. From the root up:
- Major triad: root, major 3rd, perfect 5th (C E G). Bright, stable.
- Minor triad: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th (C Eb G). Darker.
- Diminished triad: root, minor 3rd, diminished 5th (C Eb Gb). Tense, unstable.
- Augmented triad: root, major 3rd, augmented 5th (C E G#). Dreamy, unsettled.
The quality of the 3rd (major vs minor) sets the basic color; the 5th distinguishes diminished and augmented.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Inversions
chunks.text:
## Inversions
A chord is in root position when its root is the lowest note. Putting a different chord tone in the bass gives an inversion: first inversion has the 3rd in the bass (C/E), second inversion has the 5th in the bass (C/G). Inversions keep the same chord identity but change the bass line, letting harmonies connect smoothly. A seventh chord also has a third inversion, with the 7th in the bass.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Figured bass
chunks.text:
## Figured bass
Figured bass is a shorthand from the Baroque era: numbers below a bass note show the intervals above it. For triads, root position is 5/3 (usually unmarked), first inversion is 6 (often written as a "6 chord"), and second inversion is 6/4. For seventh chords: root position 7, first inversion 6/5, second inversion 4/3, third inversion 4/2 (or just 2). Roman-numeral analysis borrows these figures, so V6/5 means a dominant seventh in first inversion.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Seventh chords
chunks.text:
## Seventh chords
Adding a third above the triad's 5th gives a seventh chord, named by the triad plus the kind of 7th:
- Dominant 7th (e.g. G7): major triad + minor 7th. Restless; wants to resolve to the tonic.
- Major 7th (Cmaj7): major triad + major 7th. Lush, calm.
- Minor 7th (Dm7): minor triad + minor 7th. Smooth, mellow.
- Minor-major 7th (CmM7): minor triad + major 7th. Tense, cinematic.
- Augmented 7th and others are rarer variants.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Diminished and half-diminished sevenths
chunks.text:
## Diminished and half-diminished sevenths
- Half-diminished 7th (written m7b5 or ø7, e.g. Bm7b5): diminished triad + minor 7th. The ii chord of a minor key; leads toward the dominant.
- Fully diminished 7th (dim7 or °7, e.g. B°7): diminished triad + diminished 7th. Built of stacked minor thirds, symmetrical, and very tense — a common pivot for modulation because any of its notes can act as the root.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Extended chords (9, 11, 13)
chunks.text:
## Extended chords (9, 11, 13)
Stacking further thirds above a seventh chord gives extensions. A 9th adds the note a 9th above the root (a 2nd, up an octave); an 11th adds the 4th; a 13th adds the 6th. So C9 is a dominant 7th plus a 9th, Cmaj9 is a major 7th plus a 9th, Dm11 stacks up to the 11th. Extensions add richness and are central to jazz; in practice players omit notes (often the 5th, sometimes the root) to keep the chord playable.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Suspended and added-tone chords
chunks.text:
## Suspended and added-tone chords
A suspended chord replaces the 3rd with a neighbor: sus4 uses the 4th (Csus4 = C F G), sus2 uses the 2nd (Csus2 = C D G). With no 3rd, they sound open and unresolved, often resolving to the plain triad. An added-tone chord keeps the 3rd and adds a note without the intervening thirds: Cadd9 is a triad plus the 9th, with no 7th (unlike C9).

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Slash chords
chunks.text:
## Slash chords
A slash chord names the chord on the left and the bass note on the right: C/G means a C major triad played over a G in the bass. The bass note may be a chord tone (an inversion, like C/E) or a non-chord tone for a specific bass line. Slash chords are common in pop and folk lead sheets and chord charts.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Reading chord symbols
chunks.text:
## Reading chord symbols
In lead-sheet and chord-chart notation: a bare letter is major (C); "m" or "-" is minor (Cm); "dim" or "°" diminished; "aug" or "+" augmented; "7" a dominant 7th; "maj7" or "Δ" a major 7th; "m7b5" or "ø" half-diminished; numbers like 9/11/13 mean extensions; "sus2"/"sus4" suspensions; "add9" an added tone. The root letter can carry an accidental (Bb, F#), and a slash adds a bass note (Am7/G).

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Dynamics
chunks.text:
## Dynamics
Dynamics are the loudness of the music, marked with Italian abbreviations from soft to loud: ppp (very very soft), pp (pianissimo, very soft), p (piano, soft), mp (mezzo-piano, medium soft), mf (mezzo-forte, medium loud), f (forte, loud), ff (fortissimo, very loud), fff (very very loud). They are relative, not fixed volumes — mf in a lullaby is quieter than mf in a march.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Changing dynamics
chunks.text:
## Changing dynamics
A crescendo (cresc., or an opening hairpin) means gradually get louder; a diminuendo or decrescendo (dim., decresc., or a closing hairpin) means gradually get softer. A sudden strong accent on one note is sforzando (sfz). These shaping marks are what turn correct notes into expressive phrasing.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Articulation
chunks.text:
## Articulation
Articulation tells you how to attack and release notes:
- Staccato (a dot above the note): short and detached.
- Legato (a slur connecting notes): smooth and connected, no gap between notes.
- Accent (a > mark): emphasize, play that note stronger.
- Tenuto (a short line): hold the note for its full value, slightly stressed.
- Marcato (a ^ mark): strongly accented and somewhat detached.
- Fermata (a "bird's-eye" ⌐ over the note): hold longer than written, at the performer's discretion.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Ornaments
chunks.text:
## Ornaments
Ornaments are decorative notes added to a main note:
- Trill (tr): rapidly alternate the written note with the note above.
- Mordent: a quick single alternation with the note above (upper) or below (lower).
- Turn: a four-note figure circling the main note — above, the note, below, the note.
- Grace note (acciaccatura/appoggiatura): a small quick note played just before the main note, "crushed in" or leaning on the beat.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Common expression terms
chunks.text:
## Common expression terms
Italian terms shape mood and motion: dolce (sweetly), cantabile (in a singing style), espressivo (expressively), legato (smoothly), marcato (marked, accented), rubato (with flexible, "robbed" timing), ritardando/rit. (gradually slowing), accelerando/accel. (gradually speeding up), a tempo (return to the original speed), fine (the end), D.C. al fine (repeat from the top to "fine").

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Notes and the chromatic scale
chunks.text:
## Notes and the chromatic scale
Western music uses twelve pitches per octave, called the chromatic scale: C, C#/Db, D, D#/Eb, E, F, F#/Gb, G, G#/Ab, A, A#/Bb, B, then C again an octave higher. The seven "natural" letter names (A–G) are the white keys on a piano; the five sharps/flats between them are the black keys. There is no black key between E–F and B–C, so those pairs are only a semitone apart.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Semitones, tones, and octaves
chunks.text:
## Semitones, tones, and octaves
A semitone (half step) is the smallest distance in standard Western music — one key to the very next key on a piano, black or white. A whole tone (whole step) is two semitones. An octave is twelve semitones; two notes an octave apart share the same letter name and sound "the same but higher/lower" because one vibrates at exactly twice the frequency of the other.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Accidentals and enharmonics
chunks.text:
## Accidentals and enharmonics
A sharp (#) raises a note by a semitone; a flat (b) lowers it by a semitone; a natural (♮) cancels a previous sharp or flat. A double sharp (x) raises by two semitones, a double flat (bb) lowers by two. Two names for the same pitch are called enharmonic equivalents — for example C# and Db are the same key, as are F# and Gb. Which spelling you use depends on the key and the harmony.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Intervals
chunks.text:
## Intervals
An interval is the distance between two pitches, named by a number (how many letter names it spans) and a quality. Counting is inclusive: C up to E spans C-D-E = a third. Qualities are perfect (P), major (M), minor (m), augmented (A), and diminished (d). Unisons, fourths, fifths, and octaves can be perfect; seconds, thirds, sixths, and sevenths can be major or minor.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Interval sizes in semitones
chunks.text:
## Interval sizes in semitones
Unison 0, minor 2nd 1, major 2nd 2, minor 3rd 3, major 3rd 4, perfect 4th 5, tritone (augmented 4th / diminished 5th) 6, perfect 5th 7, minor 6th 8, major 6th 9, minor 7th 10, major 7th 11, octave 12. A major interval made one semitone smaller becomes minor; a perfect or major interval made one semitone larger becomes augmented; a perfect or minor interval made one semitone smaller becomes diminished.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Consonance and dissonance
chunks.text:
## Consonance and dissonance
Consonant intervals sound stable and restful: unisons, octaves, perfect fifths and fourths (perfect consonances), and major/minor thirds and sixths (imperfect consonances). Dissonant intervals sound tense and want to resolve: major/minor seconds and sevenths, and the tritone. Dissonance is not "bad" — it creates the motion and release that drives music forward.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: The staff, clefs, and middle C
chunks.text:
## The staff, clefs, and middle C
Music is written on a staff of five lines and four spaces. A clef fixes which pitches the lines and spaces represent. The treble clef (G clef) is used for higher instruments and the right hand at the piano; its lines from bottom to top are E-G-B-D-F and its spaces spell F-A-C-E. The bass clef (F clef) is for lower pitches and the left hand; its lines are G-B-D-F-A and its spaces A-C-E-G. Middle C sits on a ledger line just below the treble staff and just above the bass staff, linking the two in the grand staff used for piano.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Ledger lines
chunks.text:
## Ledger lines
When a note is too high or low for the staff, short ledger lines extend the staff to hold it. Middle C on a single treble staff sits on the first ledger line below; the C two octaves above middle C sits on ledger lines above the treble staff.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Key signatures
chunks.text:
## Key signatures
A key signature is the group of sharps or flats written at the start of each line, applying to every octave of those notes throughout the piece. It tells you the key. Sharps always appear in the order F, C, G, D, A, E, B (mnemonic "Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle"); flats appear in the reverse order, B, E, A, D, G, C, F.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: The circle of fifths
chunks.text:
## The circle of fifths
The circle of fifths arranges keys by ascending perfect fifths, each adding one sharp: C (0), G (1#), D (2#), A (3#), E (4#), B (5#), F# (6#). Going the other way by fifths (descending, or ascending fourths) adds flats: C (0), F (1b), Bb (2b), Eb (3b), Ab (4b), Db (5b), Gb (6b). It is the map of how keys relate: neighboring keys differ by only one accidental and sound closely related, which is why music often modulates to the key a fifth away.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Finding a major key from its signature
chunks.text:
## Finding a major key from its signature
For sharp keys, the major key is one semitone above the last sharp: with F#-C#-G# the last sharp is G#, so the key is A major. For flat keys, the major key is the second-to-last flat: with Bb-Eb-Ab the second-to-last is Eb, so the key is Eb major. (F major, one flat, is the exception you simply memorize.)

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: What this app engraves
chunks.text:
## What this app engraves
This app writes engraved staves from ABC notation in its Score cells, and renders text chord charts in its Cifra (chord-chart) cells. When a learner asks how to write something on the staff here, answer in ABC; when they ask about a chord chart, answer in plain chord-over-lyric text.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC header fields
chunks.text:
## ABC header fields
An ABC tune starts with header fields, one per line: X: the tune number (X:1), T: the title, C: the composer, M: the meter (M:4/4, or M:C for common time), L: the default note length (L:1/4 means a bare letter is a quarter note; L:1/8 an eighth), Q: the tempo (Q:1/4=120), and K: the key, which also ends the header (K:C, K:Gm, K:D). Everything after K: is music.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC pitches and octaves
chunks.text:
## ABC pitches and octaves
Letters C D E F G A B are the notes of the octave starting at middle C. Lowercase c d e f g a b are the octave above. A comma lowers an octave (C, is below middle C) and an apostrophe raises one (c' is high). Accidentals go before the note: ^ is sharp (^F = F#), _ is flat (_B = Bb), = is natural. The key signature applies automatically, so you only mark accidentals that depart from it.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC durations and rests
chunks.text:
## ABC durations and rests
With the default length set by L:, a number multiplies and a slash divides: C2 is twice the default length, C/ or C/2 is half, C3/2 is dotted. A rest is z (visible) or x (invisible); Z is a multi-measure rest. So in L:1/8, "C2 D2 E4" is two eighths' worth, two eighths' worth, then a half-note's worth.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC barlines and repeats
chunks.text:
## ABC barlines and repeats
A single barline is |, a final barline |], a double barline ||. Repeats use |: to start and :| to end; first and second endings are written [1 and [2 after the repeat. Example: |: C D E F :| repeats that bar.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: A minimal ABC example
chunks.text:
## A minimal ABC example
A full simple tune:
X:1
T:C major scale
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
C D E F | G A B c |
This engraves an ascending C major scale across two bars.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC chords, ties, and lyrics
chunks.text:
## ABC chords, ties, and lyrics
Notes played together (a chord) go in square brackets: [CEG] is a C major triad. A tie is a hyphen between two notes of the same pitch (C- C). Chord symbols above the staff go in double quotes before the note: "C"C "G7"G. Lyrics go on a w: line below the music, with syllables aligned to notes: w: Twin-kle twin-kle.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: Chord charts (Cifra cells)
chunks.text:
## Chord charts (Cifra cells)
A chord chart is plain text with chord names placed above the words, the format used on lead sheets and songbook/"cifra" sites. Write the chord names on their own line directly above the lyric line they apply to, lining up each chord over the syllable where it changes. The app reads standard chord symbols (C, Am, G7, Dsus4, F/C) and can transpose the whole chart up or down by semitones, so you write it once in any key.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Diatonic triads of a major key
chunks.text:
## Diatonic triads of a major key
Building a triad on each degree of a major scale, using only notes in the key, gives a fixed pattern of qualities, labeled with Roman numerals (uppercase = major, lowercase = minor, ° = diminished): I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°. In C major: C, Dm, Em, F, G, Am, B°. The uppercase/lowercase tells you the chord quality at a glance.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Diatonic triads of a minor key
chunks.text:
## Diatonic triads of a minor key
In natural minor the pattern is i, ii°, III, iv, v, VI, VII. In A minor: Am, B°, C, Dm, Em, F, G. In practice the 7th is often raised (harmonic minor) so the dominant becomes major (V) with a leading tone, giving the strong V–i pull; this also turns vii into a diminished vii°.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Harmonic function
chunks.text:
## Harmonic function
Diatonic chords group into three functions that explain how progressions move:
- Tonic (T): I and vi (and iii) — home, rest.
- Predominant / subdominant (PD): IV and ii — departure, setting up the dominant.
- Dominant (D): V and vii° — maximum tension, pulling back to tonic.
The basic cycle is Tonic → Predominant → Dominant → Tonic. Most common progressions are variations on that journey.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Cadences
chunks.text:
## Cadences
A cadence is a harmonic punctuation mark at the end of a phrase:
- Perfect authentic cadence (PAC): V–I with both chords in root position and the tonic in the top voice. The strongest, most final close.
- Imperfect authentic cadence (IAC): V–I that is weaker (an inversion, or not the tonic on top).
- Half cadence: a phrase ending on V — it sounds unfinished, like a comma, expecting more.
- Plagal cadence: IV–I, the "Amen" cadence; gentle and conclusive.
- Deceptive cadence: V–vi instead of the expected V–I; the resolution is dodged, prolonging the music.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Common progressions
chunks.text:
## Common progressions
- I–IV–V(–I): the backbone of countless folk, blues, and rock songs.
- I–V–vi–IV: the "four-chord" pop progression behind a huge number of hits.
- ii–V–I: the fundamental jazz cadence; in C major that is Dm7–G7–Cmaj7.
- I–vi–IV–V: the "50s" doo-wop progression.
- vi–IV–I–V: another hugely popular pop rotation of the same chords.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: The twelve-bar blues
chunks.text:
## The twelve-bar blues
The twelve-bar blues is a 12-measure form built on I, IV, and V (often as dominant 7th chords). A standard layout by bar: I-I-I-I / IV-IV-I-I / V-IV-I-V. The final V (the "turnaround") leads back to the top. It underpins blues, early rock and roll, and much of jazz.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Secondary dominants
chunks.text:
## Secondary dominants
A secondary dominant is a dominant chord borrowed from another key to tonicize (temporarily point at) a chord other than the tonic. It is written V/x — "V of x." For example V/V in C major is D7, which resolves to G (the V), making that arrival feel stronger. Secondary dominants add chromatic color and momentum without leaving the key for long.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Borrowed chords and modal mixture
chunks.text:
## Borrowed chords and modal mixture
Modal mixture (borrowing) takes chords from the parallel key — usually borrowing minor-key chords into a major key. Common examples in major are the minor iv, the bVII, and the bVI. Borrowing darkens or warms a passage while keeping the same tonic, and is everywhere in pop, film, and rock harmony.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Beat, meter, and time signatures
chunks.text:
## Beat, meter, and time signatures
The beat is the steady pulse you tap your foot to. Meter groups beats into repeating bars. A time signature shows this with two numbers: the top is how many beats per bar, the bottom is which note value gets one beat (4 = quarter note, 8 = eighth note, 2 = half note). So 4/4 is four quarter-note beats per bar, 3/4 is three.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Simple vs compound meter
chunks.text:
## Simple vs compound meter
In simple meter each beat divides into two (4/4, 3/4, 2/4). In compound meter each beat divides into three, and the time signature's top number is a multiple of three counting the divisions: 6/8 is two beats of three eighth-notes each, 9/8 is three such beats, 12/8 is four. So 6/8 feels like two big "dotted-quarter" beats, not six even ones. Common signatures: 4/4 (common time), 3/4 (waltz), 2/4 (march), 6/8 (compound duple), 12/8 (slow blues/ballad).

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Note durations
chunks.text:
## Note durations
Each note value is half the length of the one before it: whole note (4 beats in 4/4), half note (2), quarter note (1), eighth note (½), sixteenth note (¼), thirty-second (⅛). Rests mirror these durations exactly but mean silence. Beaming joins the flags of eighth and shorter notes into groups that show the beat, making rhythm easier to read.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Dots and ties
chunks.text:
## Dots and ties
A dot after a note adds half its value again: a dotted half note = half + quarter = 3 beats; a dotted quarter = quarter + eighth = 1½ beats. A second dot adds half of the first dot's value. A tie connects two notes of the same pitch into one sustained sound, used to hold a note across a barline or to make durations a single value can't write.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Tuplets and triplets
chunks.text:
## Tuplets and triplets
A tuplet squeezes an "irregular" number of notes into the time of a normal grouping. The most common is the triplet: three notes in the time of two of the same value (an eighth-note triplet fills one quarter-note beat with three even notes). Duplets and quadruplets do the reverse in compound meter; quintuplets fit five, and so on.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Syncopation
chunks.text:
## Syncopation
Syncopation places accents off the expected strong beats — on weak beats or between beats (the offbeats) — creating a sense of push, groove, or surprise. Ties, rests on strong beats, and accented upbeats all produce syncopation. It is central to jazz, funk, Latin, and most popular music.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Swing
chunks.text:
## Swing
Swing is a performance feel where pairs of eighth notes are played long-short rather than evenly, roughly as the first two notes of a triplet. It is usually written as plain eighth notes with a "swing" instruction rather than spelled out. Swing gives jazz and blues their characteristic lilt.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Tempo
chunks.text:
## Tempo
Tempo is the speed of the beat, measured in beats per minute (BPM). Traditional Italian terms give approximate ranges: Largo (very slow, ~40–60), Adagio (slow, ~66–76), Andante (walking, ~76–108), Moderato (moderate, ~108–120), Allegro (fast, ~120–168), Presto (very fast, ~168–200). A metronome marking like ♩ = 120 states it exactly: 120 quarter-note beats per minute.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The major scale
chunks.text:
## The major scale
A major scale follows the step pattern whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half (W W H W W W H). Starting on C gives all white keys: C D E F G A B C. The half steps fall between the 3rd–4th and 7th–8th degrees. This pattern is the reference every other scale and mode is measured against.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Scale degree names
chunks.text:
## Scale degree names
Each degree of a major or minor scale has a function name: 1 tonic, 2 supertonic, 3 mediant, 4 subdominant, 5 dominant, 6 submediant, 7 leading tone (when a semitone below the tonic) or subtonic (when a whole tone below, as in natural minor). The tonic is home; the dominant (5) creates the strongest pull back to it; the leading tone (7) "leans" up into the tonic.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Natural minor scale
chunks.text:
## Natural minor scale
The natural minor scale follows W H W W H W W. A natural minor is all white keys: A B C D E F G A. Compared with the major scale of the same starting note (parallel major), natural minor has a lowered 3rd, 6th, and 7th (b3, b6, b7), which gives it its darker sound.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Harmonic and melodic minor
chunks.text:
## Harmonic and melodic minor
Natural minor lacks a leading tone, weakening the pull to the tonic, so two variants raise the 7th. Harmonic minor raises the 7th by a semitone (A B C D E F G# A), creating a strong leading tone but an awkward augmented-2nd gap between the 6th and 7th. Melodic minor raises both the 6th and 7th ascending (A B C D E F# G# A) to smooth that gap, and traditionally reverts to natural minor descending.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Relative major and minor
chunks.text:
## Relative major and minor
Every major key shares its key signature with a minor key a minor third below — its relative minor, built on the 6th degree of the major. C major and A minor both have no sharps or flats; G major and E minor both have one sharp. They use the same notes but treat a different note as home.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Parallel major and minor
chunks.text:
## Parallel major and minor
Parallel keys share the same tonic but differ in quality: C major and C minor. Switching between them (borrowing chords or shifting mode) is a common expressive device. Unlike relatives, parallels have different key signatures (C major 0 accidentals, C minor 3 flats).

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The modes
chunks.text:
## The modes
The seven modes are the major scale started on each of its seven degrees, each with a distinct character:
- Ionian (1st degree) = the major scale.
- Dorian (2nd) = minor with a raised 6th; jazzy, folk.
- Phrygian (3rd) = minor with a lowered 2nd; Spanish, dark.
- Lydian (4th) = major with a raised 4th; bright, dreamy.
- Mixolydian (5th) = major with a lowered 7th; bluesy, rock.
- Aeolian (6th) = the natural minor scale.
- Locrian (7th) = diminished, with a lowered 2nd and 5th; unstable, rare.
The white-key modes are C Ionian, D Dorian, E Phrygian, F Lydian, G Mixolydian, A Aeolian, B Locrian.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Pentatonic scales
chunks.text:
## Pentatonic scales
A pentatonic scale has five notes per octave. The major pentatonic is the major scale without its 4th and 7th (C D E G A) — no semitones, so nothing clashes, which makes it forgiving for improvising. The minor pentatonic is the natural minor without its 2nd and 6th (A C D E G); it is the backbone of rock, blues, and pop soloing.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The blues scale
chunks.text:
## The blues scale
The blues scale is the minor pentatonic plus an added chromatic "blue note," the flat 5th: A C D Eb E G. That extra note, used as a passing tone, gives blues, rock, and jazz their characteristic bent, expressive sound.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Chromatic and whole-tone scales
chunks.text:
## Chromatic and whole-tone scales
The chromatic scale uses all twelve semitones in order and has no tonic of its own; it supplies passing notes and tension. The whole-tone scale divides the octave into six equal whole steps (C D E F# G# A#), sounds ambiguous and "floating" because no note feels like home, and is associated with Impressionist composers like Debussy.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Passing Tone (PT)
chunks.text:
## Passing Tone (PT)

A passing tone is a melodic embellishment (typically a non-chord tone) that occurs between two stable tones (typically chord tones), creating stepwise motion. The typical figure is *chord tone – passing tone – chord tone*, filling in a third (see example), but two adjacent passing tones can also be used to fill in the space between two chord tones a fourth apart. A passing tone can be either accented (occurring on a strong beat or strong part of the beat) or unaccented (weak beat or weak part of the beat).

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Complete Neighbor Tone (NT)
chunks.text:
## Complete Neighbor Tone (NT)

Like the passing tone, a complete neighbor tone is a melodic embellishment that occurs between two stable tones (typically chord tones); however, a complete neighbor tone will occur between two instances of the same stable tone. Also like the passing tone, movement from the stable tone to the neighbor tone and back will always be by step. A complete neighbor can be either accented or unaccented, but unaccented is more common.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Double Neighbor Figure (DN)
chunks.text:
## Double Neighbor Figure (DN)

Like the complete neighbor figure, the double neighbor figure begins and ends on the *same* stable tone (typically a chord tone). Between those two instances of the stable tone are two embellishing tones — one a step above and the other a step below the stable tone being embellished. Though individually we may consider each of the two embellishing tones to be incomplete neighbors (below), working together in the double-neighbor figure they balance each other out and create a contiguous whole, with the overall stability of a complete neighbor. A double neighbor figure is typically unaccented.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Incomplete Neighbor Tone (INT)
chunks.text:
## Incomplete Neighbor Tone (INT)

The incomplete neighbor tone is an unaccented embellishing tone that is approached by leap and proceeds by step to an accented stable tone (typically a chord tone). Broadly speaking an incomplete neighbor tone is any embellishing tone a step away from a stable tone that proceeds or follows it (and is connected on the other side by leap), but other kinds of incomplete neighbor tones have special names and roles that follow below.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Appoggiatura (APP)
chunks.text:
## Appoggiatura (APP)

An appoggiatura is a kind of incomplete neighbor tone that is accented, approached by leap (usually up), and followed by step (usually down, but always in the opposite direction of the preceding leap) to a more stable tone (typically a chord tone).

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Escape Tone (ESC)
chunks.text:
## Escape Tone (ESC)

An escape tone, or *echappée*, is a kind of incomplete neighbor tone that is unaccented, preceded by step (usually up) from a chord tone, and followed by leap (usually down, but always in the opposite direction of the preceding step).

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Anticipation (ANT)
chunks.text:
## Anticipation (ANT)

An anticipation is essentially an otherwise stable tone that comes too early. An anticipation is typically a non-chord tone that will occur immediately before a change of harmony, and it will be followed on that change of harmony by the same note, now a chord tone of the new harmony. It is typically found at the ends of phrases and larger formal units.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Syncopation (SYN)
chunks.text:
## Syncopation (SYN)

Syncopation occurs when a rhythmic pattern that typically occurs on strong beats or strong parts of the beat occurs instead on weak beats or weak parts of the beat. Like the anticipation, the syncopated note is an early arrival — it tends to belong to the chord on the following beat. Unlike the anticipation, the syncopation is tied into a note in that chord; it is not rearticulated. Rather than anticipating a note in the chord that follows, a syncopation is simply an early arrival.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Suspension (SUS)
chunks.text:
## Suspension (SUS)

A suspension is formed of three critical parts: the *preparation* (accented or unaccented), the *suspension* itself (accented), and the *resolution* (unaccented). The preparation is a chord tone (consonance). The suspension is *the same note* as the preparation and occurs simultaneous with a change of harmony. The suspension then proceeds down by step to the resolution, which occurs over the same harmony as the suspension. The suspension is in many respects the opposite of the syncopation: if the anticipation is an early arrival of a tone belonging to the following chord, a suspension is a lingering of a chord tone belonging to the previous chord that forces the late arrival of the new chord’s chord tone. However, in composition and improvisation, the suspension must be treated with a great deal more care than the syncopation. The most common suspensions (and their resolutions) in upper voices form the following intervallic patterns against the bass: 9–8, 7–6, 4–3. (With the exception of 9–8, the pitch class of the resolution tone should never sound in another voice simultaneous with the suspended tone.) Instead of *SUS*, it is more typical to notate the intervallic pattern in the thoroughbass figures.

chunks.title: Embellishing tones
chunks.heading: Retardation (RET)
chunks.text:
## Retardation (RET)

A retardation is essentially an upward-resolving suspension. It is almost always reserved for the final chord of a large formal division (or a movement), and it frequently appears simultaneously with a suspension (as seen in the example). Instead of *RET*, it is preferable to notate the intervallic pattern in the thoroughbass figures.

chunks.title: Harmonic functions
chunks.heading: Harmonic functions
chunks.text:
## Harmonic functions
If a musical function describes the role that a particular musical element plays in the creation of a larger musical unit, then *a harmonic function describes the role that a particular chord plays in the creating of a larger harmonic progression*. Each chord tends to occur in some musical situations more than others, to progress to some chords more than others. These tendencies work together to create meaningful harmonic progressions, which can in turn form the harmonic foundation for musical *phrases*, *themes*, and larger formal units.

Generally speaking, the function of a chord concerns the notes that belong to it (its *internal characteristics*), the chords that tend to precede and follow it, and where it tends to be employed in the course of a musical phrase.

A theory of harmonic functions is based on three fundamental principles:

- Chords are collections of scale degrees.  
- Each scale degree has its own tendencies.  
- The collective tendencies of a chord's scale degrees in combination is the chord's function.

(Note the absence of *root* and *quality* from consideration here.)

Because tendency is style-specific, the same chord can have different functions in different musical styles. For instance, the kinds of functions we find in classical music are different from those we find in pop/rock songs from the Billboard charts. And though there are some general harmonic traits that are common to most eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Western composers (what we call the "common practice"), when we look in closer detail, we find some significant differences in the way Bach, Mozart, Brahms, and others compose their harmonic progressions.

Our initial exploration of harmonic functions will engage the general "common practice" that is shared by most eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Western composers. As we explore specific genres, composers, and works within that common practice, we will have opportunity to explore the more nuanced differences between composers, as well as to move beyond common-practice Western art music to include other styles, such as pop/rock.

chunks.title: Harmonic functions
chunks.heading: The three common-practice harmonic functions
chunks.text:
## The three common-practice harmonic functions

In common-practice music, harmonies tend to cluster around three high-level categories of harmonic function. These categories are traditionally called *tonic* (**T**), *subdominant* (**S** — also called *predominant*, **P** or **PD**), and *dominant* (**D**). Each of these functions has their own characteristic scale degrees, with their own characteristic tendencies. And each of these functions tend to participate in certain kinds of chord progressions more than others.

If you are already comfortable with Roman numerals, you can generally think of **I**, **III**, and **VI** as *tonic*, **II** and **IV** as *subdominant*, and **V** and **VII** as *dominant*. (Though, as you will see below, there is more to it than that.) 

To visualize these functional categories, think of the usual triads in C major arranged on a circle of thirds. Note that each chord sits between the two triads that share the most tones in common — C major (C, E, G) sits between E minor (E, G, B) and A minor (A, C, E), both of which share two tones in common with C.

Convert these chords to Roman numerals (in C major), and we can see the functions. Since the function is determined by the tendencies of the tones that they share, and since on this graph chords are grouped together by notes they have in common, they are also grouped together by function.

*Triads arranged on the circle of thirds, labeled by harmonic functions.*

Interestingly, in common-practice music, a chord's function can be determined solely by its internal characteristics (the notes that make up the chord). This is not true of all styles. For example, in pop/rock music a **IV** chord can exhibit very different functional tendencies depending on its context. But in classical music, simply knowing the notes in a chord is enough to determine its general harmonic function and the general tendencies of that chord and its individual notes.

The syntactic properties of these functions will be covered elsewhere. What follows simply explains how to determine the function of a chord in common-practice music with greater specificity.

chunks.title: Harmonic functions
chunks.heading: Finding the function of a chord ##
chunks.text:
## Finding the function of a chord ##

Each of the three harmonic functions — *tonic* (T), *subdominant* (S), and *dominant* (D) — have characteristic scale degrees. Tonic's characteristic scale degrees are 1, 3, 5, 6, and 7. Subdominant's characteristic scale degrees are 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6. Dominant's characteristic scale degrees are 2, 4, 5, 6, and 7.

Ian Quinn (a music theorist at Yale University) further distinguishes these scale degrees, using the categories of functional *triggers*, functional *associates*, and functional *dissonances*. These categories help us understand the functional properties of chords whose scale degrees belong to more than one function, as well as how certain notes behave within a chord. They also help us understand which scale degrees are more or less characteristic of a function ― something that will help determine function when a complete chord is not present.

| function 	| triggers 	| associates 	| dissonances 	|
| :-: | :- | :- | :- |
| **T**	| 1 and 3	| 5 and 6	| 5 (if 6 is also present) and 7 |
| **S**	| 4 and 6	| 1 and 2	| 1 (if 2 is also present) and 3 |
| **D**	| 5 and 7	| 2	| 4 and 6 |

In terms of moveable-*do* solfège:

| function 	| triggers 	| associates 	| dissonances 	|
| :-: | :- | :- | :- |
| **T**	| *do* and *mi*/*me*	| *sol* and *la*/*le*	| *sol* (if *la*/*le* is also present) and *ti*/*te*
| **S**	| *fa* and *la*/*le*	| *do* and *re*	| *do* (if *re* is also present) and *mi*/*me*
| **D**	| *sol* and *ti*/*te*	| *re*	| *fa* and *la*/*le*

To determine the function of a chord, find the function that includes all the scale degrees of a chord (regardless of chromatic alterations ― that is, treat ♯4 the same as regular scale-degree 4). If more than one function contains all the scale degrees, take the function with the most triggers in the chord.

There is one exception to this (for now): a chord with scale degrees 6, 1, and 3 is a special kind of tonic chord, called a *destabilized tonic*. Quinn uses the special functional label is **Tx**, rather than simply **T**, for this chord.

Also note that because the III7 chord's scale-degrees do not wholly belong to any of the three functions, it can behave similar to **T** and **D** chords, depending on context. It is a rare chord in its diatonic form.

This handout will help you determine the function of a chord from the bass scale degree and/or the Roman numeral.

chunks.title: Harmonic functions
chunks.heading: Labeling chords ##
chunks.text:
## Labeling chords ##

There are two ways in which we will label chords according to function. The first is to label chords with Roman numerals, thoroughbass figures, and functional labels. When doing so, place the appropriate Roman numeral *below* the bass line, the thoroughbass figure *above* the bass line (since it represents the upper voices), and place a functional label **T**/**S**/**D** below the Roman numeral (no **Tx**; simply call a **VI** chord **T**). For now this label can simply apply T, S, or D to individual chords; in the future, we will alter this practice slightly in order to show functional prolongation. The first example shows individual chord functions, and the second example shows functional prolongation.

[](/Graphics/harmony/RNsIndividualFunctions.png)

[](/Graphics/harmony/RNsFunctionalProlongation.png)

The second way to label a harmonic progression is what Quinn calls *functional bass*. Functional bass symbols combine a chord's function (**T**, **S**, **D**, or **Tx**) with an Arabic numeral denoting the scale degree of its bass note. A tonic chord with *do* in the bass is **T1**, a dominant chord with *ti* in the bass is **D7**, etc. If the bass note is chromatically altered, use a **+** or **–** to denote raised or lowered (*la* and *ti* in minor do not count, since *le*, *la*, *te*, and *ti* all belong to minor, but you can use +/– for clarity if you like). And if there is a chromatically altered note anywhere in the chord, put the functional bass symbol inside square brackets: **[S6]**, **[S+4]**, **[T–7]**, etc. (See Chromatically altered subdominant chords, Applied chords, and Modal mixture for more information on common chromatically altered chords.)

[](/Graphics/harmony/FunctionalBassFunctions.png)

Quinn also advocates using what I call *interpreted functional bass*. This nomenclature uses the same symbols, but uses parentheses to denote *contrapuntal prolongation* and lower-case postscripts to explain the contrapuntal role of the embellishing chord (p for passing, n for neighbor, i for incomplete neighbor, d for divider, e for embellishing — all of these refer to the voice-leading pattern in the bass voice). Following is an example of interpreted functional bass.

[](/Graphics/harmony/InterpretedFunctionalBass.png)

In this text, we primarily use the first method of Roman numerals and (prolonged) harmonic functions, since it is the most common in North American music theory. However, functional bass can be helpful for identifying categories of chords that belong together. For example, in a dictation or transcription task, we might hear *re* in the bass but not know what specific chord it is. If context tells us it is likely a dominant chord, rather than subdominant, we can label it **D2**. This rules out **II** (a subdominant chord) but keeps open multiple dominant options like **V6/4** or **VII6** until we are able to make a final determination. Similarly, when composing, there are patterns that might take an **S4**, with the specific chord (**IV** or **II6**) determined by voice-leading rather than harmonic syntax, but where a **D4** chord (**V4/2**) would be syntactically inappropriate, regardless of voice-leading.

Thus, when referring to specific chords, we will use Roman numerals to label the chords and functional labels to interpret their role in context. When referring to broader categories of chords, we will more often use functional bass.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Diminished chord
chunks.text:
## Diminished chord
The Diminished chord (abbreviated dim) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, tritone. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Suspended second chord
chunks.text:
## Suspended second chord
The Suspended second chord (abbreviated sus2) is built from the root upward as: root, major 2nd, perfect 5th. Spelled from a C root that is C D G.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Minor chord
chunks.text:
## Minor chord
The Minor chord (abbreviated min) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Major chord
chunks.text:
## Major chord
The Major chord (abbreviated Maj) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, perfect 5th. Spelled from a C root that is C E G.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Suspended fourth chord
chunks.text:
## Suspended fourth chord
The Suspended fourth chord (abbreviated sus4) is built from the root upward as: root, perfect 4th, perfect 5th. Spelled from a C root that is C F G.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Augmented chord
chunks.text:
## Augmented chord
The Augmented chord (abbreviated aug) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, minor 6th. Spelled from a C root that is C E A♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Italian sixth chord
chunks.text:
## Italian sixth chord
The Italian sixth chord (abbreviated It6) is built from the root upward as: tritone, minor 6th. Spelled from a C root that is G♭ A♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: French sixth chord
chunks.text:
## French sixth chord
The French sixth chord (abbreviated Fr6) is built from the root upward as: root, major 2nd, tritone, minor 6th. Spelled from a C root that is C D G♭ A♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: German sixth chord
chunks.text:
## German sixth chord
The German sixth chord (abbreviated Ger6) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, tritone, minor 6th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G♭ A♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Diminished seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Diminished seventh chord
The Diminished seventh chord (abbreviated dim7) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, tritone, major 6th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G♭ A.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Minor Sixth chord
chunks.text:
## Minor Sixth chord
The Minor Sixth chord (abbreviated min6) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th, major 6th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G A.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Half-diminished seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Half-diminished seventh chord
The Half-diminished seventh chord (abbreviated ø7) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, tritone, minor 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G♭ B♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Dominant seventh flat five chord
chunks.text:
## Dominant seventh flat five chord
The Dominant seventh flat five chord (abbreviated dom7♭5) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, tritone, minor 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E G♭ B♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Minor seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Minor seventh chord
The Minor seventh chord (abbreviated min7) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th, minor 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G B♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Dominant seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Dominant seventh chord
The Dominant seventh chord (abbreviated dom7) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, minor 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E G B♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Augmented seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Augmented seventh chord
The Augmented seventh chord (abbreviated aug7) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E A♭ B♭.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Minor-major seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Minor-major seventh chord
The Minor-major seventh chord (abbreviated mM7, m/M7, m(M7)) is built from the root upward as: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E♭ G B.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Major seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Major seventh chord
The Major seventh chord (abbreviated Maj7) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E G B.

chunks.title: Chord qualities
chunks.heading: Augmented major seventh chord
chunks.text:
## Augmented major seventh chord
The Augmented major seventh chord (abbreviated augM7) is built from the root upward as: root, major 3rd, minor 6th, major 7th. Spelled from a C root that is C E A♭ B.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Major (Ionian) scale
chunks.text:
## Major (Ionian) scale
The Major (Ionian) scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, major 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E F G A B. Also known as: Major, Ionian, Ajam Shiram, Ararai, Dasrgah-e Mahur, Dastgah-e Rast Panjgah.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Natural minor (Aeolian) scale
chunks.text:
## Natural minor (Aeolian) scale
The Natural minor (Aeolian) scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D E♭ F G A♭ B♭. Also known as: Natural Minor, Aeolian, Cushak, Ezel, Geez, Mela Natabhairavi.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Harmonic minor scale
chunks.text:
## Harmonic minor scale
The Harmonic minor scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E♭ F G A♭ B. Also known as: Harmonic Minor, Maqam Bayat-e-Esfahan, Maqam Sultani Yakah, Mela Kiravani, Mischung 4, Mohammedan.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Melodic minor (ascending) scale
chunks.text:
## Melodic minor (ascending) scale
The Melodic minor (ascending) scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, major 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E♭ F G A B. Also known as: Melodic Minor, Ascending Minor, Mela Gaurimanohari, Mischung 1, Raga Deshi 2, Raga Patdip.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Dorian scale
chunks.text:
## Dorian scale
The Dorian scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, major 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D E♭ F G A B♭. Also known as: Gregorian 8, Hyojo, Mela Kharaharapriya, Mischung 5, Nam, Oshikicho.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Phrygian scale
chunks.text:
## Phrygian scale
The Phrygian scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D♭ E♭ F G A♭ B♭. Also known as: Major Inverse, Maqam Kurd, Mela Bhairavi That, Mela Hanumatodi, Ousak, Raga Bilashkhani Todi.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Lydian scale
chunks.text:
## Lydian scale
The Lydian scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, tritone, perfect 5th, major 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E G♭ G A B. Also known as: Gu, Mela Mecakalyani, Ping, Raga Kalyan That, Raga Shuddh Kalyan.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Mixolydian scale
chunks.text:
## Mixolydian scale
The Mixolydian scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, major 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D E F G A B♭. Also known as: Gregorian 2, Gregorian 7, Mela Harikamboji, Mischung 3, Raga Balahamsa, Raga Harini.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Locrian scale
chunks.text:
## Locrian scale
The Locrian scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, tritone, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D♭ E♭ F G♭ A♭ B♭. Also known as: Makam Lami, Pien Chih, Yishtabach.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Major pentatonic scale
chunks.text:
## Major pentatonic scale
The Major pentatonic scale has 5 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 6th. Spelled from C: C D E G A. Also known as: Major Pentatonic, Ghana Pentatonic 2, Gong, Man Jue, Peruvian Major Pentatonic, Raga Bhopali.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Minor pentatonic scale
chunks.text:
## Minor pentatonic scale
The Minor pentatonic scale has 5 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C E♭ F G B♭. Also known as: Minor Pentatonic, Bati, Blues Minor Pentatonic, Gu Xian, Jia Zhong, Lai Noi.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Blues scale
chunks.text:
## Blues scale
The Blues scale has 6 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, tritone, perfect 5th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C E♭ F G♭ G B♭. Also known as: Blues Hexatonic, Raga Nileshwari.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Whole tone scale
chunks.text:
## Whole tone scale
The Whole tone scale has 6 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, tritone, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D E G♭ A♭ B♭. Also known as: Whole-Tone, Anhemitonic Hexatonic, Hexatonic, Messiaen 1st Mode, Raga Gopriya, Raga Sahera.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Octatonic (diminished) scale
chunks.text:
## Octatonic (diminished) scale
The Octatonic (diminished) scale has 8 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, perfect 4th, tritone, minor 6th, major 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E♭ F G♭ A♭ A B. Also known as: Diminished, Messiaen 2nd Mode Inverse, Modus Conjunctus, Octatonic, Whole-Tone Diminished.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Phrygian dominant scale
chunks.text:
## Phrygian dominant scale
The Phrygian dominant scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 2nd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 6th, minor 7th. Spelled from C: C D♭ E F G A♭ B♭. Also known as: Phrygian Dominant, Alhijaz, Avaha, Ahava Rabba, Dorico Flamenco, Freygish.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Harmonic major scale
chunks.text:
## Harmonic major scale
The Harmonic major scale has 7 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, major 2nd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, perfect 5th, minor 6th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D E F G A♭ B. Also known as: Harmonic Major, Mela Sarasangi, Mischung 2, Raga Haripriya, Segiah, Tabahaniotiko.

chunks.title: Scales
chunks.heading: Chromatic scale
chunks.text:
## Chromatic scale
The Chromatic scale has 12 notes. From the root its intervals are: root, minor 2nd, major 2nd, minor 3rd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, tritone, perfect 5th, minor 6th, major 6th, minor 7th, major 7th. Spelled from C: C D♭ D E♭ E F G♭ G A♭ A B♭ B.
